snippet.title: IBM CEO Arvind Krishna On The Future Of Big Blue
snippet.description:
Arvind Krishna began his career with IBM in 1990 and has been the CEO of IBM since April 2020 and Chairman since January 2021. Following his IBM Think 2022 Keynote in Boston, that I attended, Arvind sat down for a live round table with industry analysts to address a range of subjects in an open question format. In this article, I will paraphrase Arvind's detailed and lengthy responses, hopefully providing clues to the future of Big Blue. Technology is an undisputed source of competitive advantageToday, few CEOs think technology doesn't matter. Most CEOs will say that technology is the single most protected line item even in a down market and a bad economy because technology will provide a sustainable advantage. Government leaders all want a robust technology industry to increase gross domestic product (GDP) at the country level. I would concur based on my experience in talking with CEOs. Treating IT as a cost of doing business will ultimately lead to a loss of competitiveness in the marketplace. Arvind is a technologist at heart, so it is not surprising that he has placed technology and the necessary ecosystem of partners at the heart of the business model. I believe that, in the new wave of tech CEOs, technology is respected more than ever and is a benefit to IBM. Creativity and co-creation are criticalToday's IBM is more open to partnerships across the stack, based on the reality that no single company has all the expertise and technology to meet customers' needs. IBM wants partners to succeed but will still play a lesser but critical role in providing innovative technology. IBM plans to focus on key technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI), hybrid cloud, quantum computing, and blockchain. The rise of the cloud drives this significant change in strategy. The days of selling software, hardware, and consulting in one package are in the rear-view mirror for IBM. Cloud computing meant disruption across every layer of the stack. We all know the acronyms now SaaS (software-as-a-service), PaaS (platform-as-a-service), and IaaS (infrastructure-as-a-service). In a follow-up 1:1 with Arvind, he talked about products taking a front-row seat to consulting. Since his arrival, the company has shifted from 70% consulting- 30% products to 30% consulting- 70% product. This is a massive shift, and, to me, a "product guy", says everything. IBM's newly announced deals with AWS and SAP represent the theme of partner co-creation well. McDonalds is a good IBM client example, where IBM went so far as to acquire the tech arm of McDonalds, MCD Tech, to facilitate the new drive-through solution. The hybrid cloud has come of ageFour years ago, there was general skepticism around the hybrid cloud with a preference for a single public cloud provider. That has changed as customers tried to avoid vendor lock-in, security, regulations, cost of moving data, and the desire to have a strategic architecture to address reality and stay flexible for the future.


All data is taken from the source: http://forbes.com
Article Link: https://www.forbes.com/sites/patrickmoorhead/2022/05/16/ibm-ceo-arvind-krishna-on-the-future-of-big-blue/


#ibm #newstrump #bbcnewstoday #newstodayinusa #newstodayupdate #newstodaydonaldtrump #

snippet.title: A conversation with IBM Chairman and CEO Arvind Krishna
snippet.description: In 1982, Arvind Krishna was convinced “the whole world would go digital as soon as it could.” Little did he know how right he was—and that he would be leading the company that helped make that possible. Krishna, the Chairman and CEO of IBM, joined Zoetis CEO Kristin Peck on her latest episode of On Purpose to talk about where technology is headed, what that means for businesses, and the surprising global challenge that galvanized the company’s evolution.

snippet.title: Technology is a deflationary answer to today's macro struggles, says IBM CEO Arvind Krishna
snippet.description:
IBM CEO Arvind Krishna joins 'TechCheck' to discuss the macro strains that are leading to volatility, the role technological innovation has in fighting inflation and intellectual property concerns in China. snippet.title: Fire-side chat with Arvind Krishna | India Business Conference 2022 at Columbia Business School
snippet.description:
Fireside Chat with Arvind Krishna, Chairman & CEO, IBM Global, moderated by Professor Medini Singh, Columbia Business School at the 17th India Business Conference.

About the conference:
The India Business Conference is the premier India-focused annual conference organized by Columbia Business School in New York City. It is a powerful forum that inspires thought-leadership and generates discussions around the business, social, political, and creative undercurrents that permeate Indian life. 

The 17th edition of the conference was held virtually and was brought to you by the students of Columbia Business School, powered by SBI.

snippet.title: Our Group CEO Peng Xiao joins Arvind Krishna, Chairman and CEO at IBM, in ADIPEC Panel Discussion
snippet.description:
Last week, at ADIPEC, our Group CEO, @Peng Xiao joined @Arvind Krishna, Chairman and CEO at IBM and @Sophie Hildebrand, CTO at ADNOC in a highly-compelling panel discussion about how businesses can be transformed through the power of #AI.

In the panel, Peng shed more light on how our operating companies leverage AI as a tool to power progress – not just for businesses but for society at large.

snippet.title: Think 2022 Panel Discussion w/ Bryan Young & Arvind Krishna, CEO of IBM
snippet.description:
@IBM  CEO Arvind Krishna onstage at Think 2022.  He discusses how their partnership allows him to build the #HeLPnetwork and Kev, a virtual artificial intelligence to help lenders address systemic biases in the home buying process. 

More than 20,000 Potential homebuyers have helped Home Lending Pal and the mortgage industry build artificial intelligence, blockchain, and gamification elements to make buying a home more accessible and equitable for all using the HLP marketplace and app.
